name: Under Armour, Inc.

profile.description: Under Armour, Inc. designs, develops, markets, and distributes performance apparel, footwear, and accessories for men, women, and youth under brands including UNDER ARMOUR, HOVR, HEATGEAR, and COLDGEAR. The company sells through wholesale channels, mono-branded retail stores, and e-commerce. Under CEO/founder Kevin Plank (returned April 2024), the company is executing a multi-year brand turnaround including 25% SKU reduction, channel cleanup, and refocus on performance athletic identity. Founded in 1996 and headquartered in Baltimore, Maryland.

innovation.label: WEAK
innovation.analysis: Track Record (0/3): Under Armour has not launched a successful new product or business model transformation in the last 5 years. The company has been in restructuring mode — contracting revenue from $5.9B (FY2023) to $5.0B (FY2026) while exiting unprofitable channels and reducing SKUs by 25% [1][2]. This is brand repair, not innovation. AI Deployment (1/2): UA has invested in AI-driven product design including the Fox 1 basketball shoe and maintains the Connected Fitness platform (MapMyFitness), but no quantified AI-driven revenue or cost savings have been reported [3]. Market Share (1/2): Under Armour remains a meaningful player in premium performance athletic wear, behind Nike and Adidas, but has been losing share as On Running and Hoka gain momentum with trend-conscious consumers [3]. Product Pipeline (1/2): The brand reset under Plank targets a return to performance-focused products with specific initiatives in footwear, but no specific next-gen product launches with named dates and revenue visibility were identified beyond general turnaround strategy [2]. R&D Intensity (0/1): R&D spending is below Nike and Adidas as a percentage of revenue, with the company focused on cost reduction rather than innovation investment during the turnaround.

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): Under Armour's mission 'to make all athletes better through passion, design, and the relentless pursuit of innovation' is specific, distinctive, and connects the company to a genuine purpose beyond profit [2]. It differentiates from generic corporate missions and clearly shapes product strategy. However, years of brand drift under prior CEO Patrik Frisk diluted the mission's connection to execution, preventing a score of 4. Community Impact (1/2): Under Armour has partnerships with elite athletes and sports programs that create genuine community impact in the athletic world, but no distinctive large-scale charitable foundation with specific dollar amounts was identified [2]. Employee Retention (0/2): Multiple waves of restructuring, CEO changes (Plank to Frisk to Plank), and workforce reduction from 16,000+ to 6,200 employees indicate significant organizational instability and disruption — retention cannot score positively in this environment [1]. Insider Buying (1/2): Four insider share purchases were reported over the past six months alongside the Q4 FY2026 earnings, and the $500M buyback program signals management conviction in the recovery, earning a point for mixed but present insider activity [1].

infrastructure.label: WEAK
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored as a Growth stock. Q4 FY2026 revenue of $1.17B declined 1% YoY, with North America down 7% while international rose 10% [1]. Full-year FY2026 revenue of $4.97B represents a -0.8% decline. D/E of 137% is above the 70% target (score 0.71). ROE of -30% is below the 0% floor, scoring 0 on 2x weight. Revenue growth YoY of -0.8% is between floor and target (score 0.64). Revenue 3-year CAGR of -5.6% is below the -5% floor, scoring 0 on 2x weight. Forward P/E of 23.4 is above the 20 target but well below the 60 floor (score 0.92). Profit margin of -10% is below the 0% floor, scoring 0. PEG of 1.84 is between the 1.0 target and 3.0 floor (score 0.58 on 2x weight). Weighted sum: 3.42/10 → Infrastructure = 3. Liquidity: $49M daily turnover passes the $1M floor. FY2027 EPS guidance of $0.08-0.12 is below consensus, confirming continued financial weakness [1].

ai_resilience.label: MODERATE
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (6/9): Under Armour's core business — designing, manufacturing, and marketing physical athletic apparel and footwear — involves tangible products, brand identity, and athletic endorsements that AI cannot replace [3]. However, AI poses real competitive threats: Nike (190M+ app users) and Adidas are deploying AI-driven personalization, product design, and supply chain optimization that could systematically widen their competitive advantage. Generative AI tools for product design could lower barriers to entry for new competitors. S&P downgraded UA's credit rating to BB-, and analysts note the brand's 'underdog' positioning feels outdated against agile rivals like On Running and Hoka [3]. AI is not an existential threat to athletic apparel, but competitors with superior data assets can use AI more effectively to capture share. AI Opportunity (0/1): Under Armour has invested in AI-driven design and the Connected Fitness platform, but no quantifiable AI-specific revenue has been reported. These remain exploratory rather than revenue-generating.

infinite_game.summary: Under Armour's return of founder Kevin Plank as CEO in April 2024 represents an attempt to reconnect with the founding athletic performance identity — a potentially infinite game restoration. Plank has stated the 'most disruptive phase of the turnaround is over' and is shifting focus to stabilization [2]. However, the company has been in reactive restructuring for five years, the workforce has been cut from 16,000+ to 6,200, and the brand's aspirational positioning has been blurred by years of over-expansion. The question is whether Plank can rebuild what was damaged.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): Under Armour's original cause — making athletes better through performance innovation — is a genuine, specific purpose that Plank articulated clearly in the founding era [2]. The return of Plank represents an attempt to restore this cause after years of drift under prior CEO Patrik Frisk. The 25% SKU reduction specifically targets anything that doesn't 'demonstrably improve athletic performance,' showing the cause actively shaping strategic decisions. However, the cause hasn't been consistently validated through decades of execution — the 2019-2024 drift period demonstrates fragility.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (0/2): Multiple waves of restructuring, CEO changes (Plank to Frisk to Plank), and dramatic workforce reduction from 16,000+ to 6,200 employees indicate a deeply unstable organizational environment [1][2]. Senior leadership changes announced in January 2026 added further organizational disruption. Negative operating income, net loss of $201M in FY2025, and continued revenue contraction create financial stress that pressures team morale and psychological safety. This is a low-trust environment by any measure.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): Under Armour explicitly positions Nike and Adidas as worthy rivals whose strengths have exposed UA's weaknesses in brand storytelling, global distribution, and digital ecosystems [3]. Plank's return narrative is explicitly about learning from what competitors do well and improving UA's own execution. The company also acknowledges On Running and Hoka as competitors whose agility reveals UA's sluggishness — a textbook infinite game competitive mindset.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): Under Armour has undergone significant restructuring — exiting unprofitable wholesale channels, reducing SKUs 25%, disposing of non-core assets, and accepting multi-year revenue contraction [2]. The brand is returning to its athletic performance roots rather than clinging to the over-expanded model. However, this is defensive retrenchment rather than a proactive strategic pivot to a new market or business model. The company is going backward to go forward rather than reinventing itself.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): Plank's return to lead the company through painful revenue contraction (FY2025 down 9.4%, FY2026 down 0.8%, FY2027 guidance signaling another tough year) in service of rebuilding brand equity demonstrates genuine long-term thinking over short-term revenue defense [1][2]. Accepting a 17% stock drop on Q4 results rather than over-promising on FY2027 guidance shows willingness to absorb near-term pain. However, the original strategic mistakes that created the need for this turnaround (over-expansion, brand dilution) were made under Plank's earlier tenure.

investment_thesis: Under Armour's bull case is a founder-led turnaround with the 'most disruptive phase' now behind it: Kevin Plank's return has stabilized the brand narrative, the 25% SKU reduction is sharpening the performance identity, and international revenue grew 10% in Q4 FY2026 with APAC up 13% [1][2]. Direct-to-consumer grew 5% with owned stores up 8%, suggesting the brand-elevation strategy is resonating in controlled channels. At $5.67/share with a $2.4B market cap and forward EPS guidance of $0.08-0.12 for FY2027, UAA trades at a deep discount to intrinsic brand value with significant option value if North American revenue stabilizes and margins recover toward historical levels.
thesis_continued: The bear case is severe: FY2026 revenue of $4.97B is down 16% from the FY2023 peak of $5.9B, with no near-term reversal visible [1]. ROE of -30%, profit margin of -10%, negative free cash flow of $528M, and $1.94B in total debt against $309M in cash create fragile financial health. S&P downgraded the credit rating to BB- citing concerns over brand strength and financial stability [3]. FY2027 EPS guidance of $0.08-0.12 is below consensus and triggered a 17% stock decline, suggesting the turnaround timeline keeps extending. Nike, On Running, and Hoka continue to take share, and tariff escalation on Asian manufacturing could compress the gross margin that is currently the only financial bright spot. Five years of restructuring without revenue stabilization tests the patience of even the most patient investors.
key_catalysts: North American revenue stabilization in FY2027 H2, demonstrating that channel cleanup and brand repositioning have stopped the share loss and set the stage for growth alongside Nike and Adidas
International business sustaining 10%+ growth with APAC and EMEA becoming consistent growth drivers that offset North American weakness and diversify the revenue base
Successful footwear product launch (building on the Fox 1 basketball shoe) that captures meaningful market share in UA's weakest segment and expands the addressable market
key_risks: Revenue decline now in its third consecutive year with FY2027 guidance signaling no inflection — continued contraction could trigger debt covenant stress on the $1.94B debt load and further credit downgrades below BB-

Tariff escalation on Asian-sourced manufacturing increasing COGS and compressing the 47.9% gross margin that is the primary financial bright spot in the turnaround, with CEO Plank citing tariff headwinds in Q4 guidance

Kevin Plank departure or leadership instability — the entire bull case requires founder-led brand restoration over 3-5 years, and any leadership disruption would restart the market's confidence clock after the January 2026 senior leadership changes already created organizational uncertainty
